---
title: "4.4 Indexed Array Traversals"
subtitle: "Array Traversals"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Array Traversals

### Visiting each element

In this lesson, we will learn how to traverse an array using a loop.

**Traversing** an array means visiting each element of the array.

We can use a loop to visit each element of an array and perform some operation on it.

This is a common operation when working with arrays.

## Index Variables

### Arithmetic inside brackets

In the last lesson, we mentioned that you can use a variable for the index of an array.

You can even do math with that index and have an arithmetic expression inside the square brackets.

```java
// highScores array declaration
int[] highScores = {10, 9, 8, 8};
// use a variable for the index
int index = 3;
// modify array value at index
highScores[index] = 11;
// print array value at index
System.out.println(highScores[index]);
System.out.println(highScores[index - 1]);
```

## Array With Index Variable {.image-fit}

### Trace the output

![Array with index variable](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-4-array-traversals/arraywithindex.png){fig-align="center" width="48%"}

What does the code print out?

The external visualizer link is omitted; the trace prompt and image are retained.

## Loops to Traverse Arrays

### Indexed traversal

**Traversing an array** or **iteration** is when repetition statements are used to access all or an ordered sequence of elements in an array.

We can traverse an array with an indexed `for` loop or `while` loop, accessing the elements using an index variable.

Start the index at `0` and loop while the index is less than the `length` of the array.

The variable `i`, short for index, is often used as the loop counter variable.

## For Loop Traversal {.image-fit}

### Counter variable as index

![For loop traversing array](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-4-array-traversals/arrayForLoop.png){fig-align="center" width="62%"}

Since this is a simple counter-controlled loop, `for` loops are used more often than `while` loops for array traversals.

## Traversal Code

### `for` and `while`

```java
int[] highScores = {10, 9, 8, 11};
for (int i = 0; i < highScores.length; i++)
{
    System.out.println(highScores[i]);
}
// Or with a while loop
int i = 0;
while (i < highScores.length)
{
    System.out.println(highScores[i]);
    i++;
}
```

## Source Note

### Data abstraction

Using a variable as the index is a powerful **data abstraction** feature.

It allows us to use loops with arrays where the loop counter variable is the index of the array.

This allows our code to generalize to work for the whole array.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: arraytrace2`

What do you think the following code will print out?

First trace through it on paper keeping track of the array and the index variable.

Then run it to see if you were right.

Then try adding your name and a friend's name to the array `names` and run the code again.

Did the code work without changing the loop?

## arraytrace2 Starter

```java
public class Test2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String[] names = {"Jamal", "Emily", "Destiny", "Mateo", "Sofia"};

        for (int i = 0; i < names.length; i++)
        {
            System.out.println(names[i]);
        }
        // TODO: Add your name and a friend's name to the array initializer list.
    }
}
```

## arraytrace2 Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The first expected output contains:

```text
Jamal
Emily
Destiny
Mateo
Sofia
```

After adding two more names, the output should have at least six lines and include the new names.

## Arrays as Objects and Parameters

### Reference values

The following code demonstrates a loop that changes the values in an array.

In this code, the array is passed as an argument to the static methods in the class.

Arrays in Java are objects.

The array variables are references to an address in memory.

Since arrays can be very large, we do not want to copy them when we pass them into methods.

## Passing Arrays to Methods

### Changes affect the original

When an array is passed as an argument to a method, the name of the array refers to its address in memory.

Therefore, any changes to the array in the method will affect the original array.

The external Java visualizer link is omitted; the source trace code is retained.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: arrayMethodTrace`

What does the following code print out?

Trace through it keeping track of the array values and the output.

Then run it to see if you are right.

Notice that the array is passed as an argument to the methods.

## arrayMethodTrace Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class ArrayLoop
{

    // What does this method do?
    public static void multAll(int[] values, int amt)
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < values.length; i++)
        {
            values[i] = values[i] * amt;
        }
    }

    // What does this method do?
    public static void printValues(int[] values)
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < values.length; i++)
        {
            System.out.println(values[i]);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] numArray = {2, 6, 7, 12, 5};
        multAll(numArray, 2);
        printValues(numArray);
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### Runestone check

The expected output from `main` is:

```text
4
12
14
24
10
```

`multAll` changes the original array because the array reference is passed to the method.

## Source Note

### Array arguments

Arrays in Java are objects.

When arrays are passed in as arguments to methods, any changes to the array in the method will affect the original array.

The array name is a reference value referring to the address of the array in memory.

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: patdonsArraySubtract`

The following method has the correct code to subtract `amt` from all the values in the array `values`, but the code is mixed up.

Drag the blocks into the correct order.

## Correct Order

### `subAll`

```java
public void subAll(int[] values, int amt)
{
    for (int i = 0; i < values.length; i++)
    {
        values[i] = values[i] - amt;
    } // end for loop
} // end method
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define traversal as visiting each element of the array
- use `i = 0; i < array.length; i++` as the indexed traversal pattern
- explain why `i` can be used inside `array[i]`
- trace `arraytrace2` before and after adding names
- explain why passing an array to a method can mutate the original
- order `subAll` with the loop header before the array update statement
:::

## End

### 4.4 Part 1 complete

Next: partial traversals and common loop errors.
